metadata.seed: Well, I'm working full time. I'm a part time student. I've just gotten engaged, and I live at home. There's just so much stress in my life. I have everything happening all at once. Just to top it all off, I failed an exam, a really big exam, and I just feel like I can't do anything anymore. I can't do anything right. I've just got so much stress, and I feel like such a disappointment, such a failure. And I've embarrassed my family by failing. I just don't know what to do anymore. I guess I've come here today to tell you, like, I want to leave uni, and I guess that's why I've come here to you today.

patient.query: Well, I'm working full time. I'm a part time student. I've just gotten engaged, and I live at home. There's just so much stress in my life. I have everything happening all at once. Just to top it all off, I failed an exam, a really big exam, and I just feel like I can't do anything anymore. I can't do anything right. I've just got so much stress, and I feel like such a disappointment, such a failure. And I've embarrassed my family by failing. I just don't know what to do anymore. I guess I've come here today to tell you, like, I want to leave uni, and I guess that's why I've come here to you today.

llm_response.response: I hear how overwhelmed you’re feeling right now—balancing a full‑time job, part‑time studies, an engagement, and a big exam failure all at once can feel like a lot to handle. It’s understandable that you’re thinking about leaving university and feeling like a disappointment; those thoughts can feel very heavy. Let’s explore what might help you feel a bit more grounded and supported as you navigate these challenges.

patient.query: Yeah, it’s just... everything feels so much right now. I mean, I thought I could handle it all, but then the exam didn’t go well, and it just feels like proof that I’m not cut out for this. Sometimes, I just want to hide away and avoid it all, like maybe that would make the pressure go away. I don’t know.

automatic_thoughts: I'm not cut out for this
I thought I could handle it all

llm_response.response:
It sounds like the exam outcome has shaken your confidence and left you feeling unsure about your fit for this path.  
What specific thoughts or evidence are leading you to believe that this proves you’re not cut out for it?  
Could there be other explanations for how the exam went that don’t hinge on your overall ability or suitability?

patient.query: I guess I just keep thinking that if I can't even do well on this one thing, then what’s the point? Like, it just feels like a sign that I should’ve chosen something else. I mean, maybe there are other reasons, like I was stressed out or didn’t study the right way, but... those thoughts just don’t stick. It’s like I’m stuck on this one failure.

triggers: not doing well on one thing
automatic_thoughts: I’m stuck on this one failure

llm_response.response: It sounds like this one setback feels like a verdict on everything you’re doing. What evidence do you have that this failure means you should have chosen something else? Perhaps this could be seen as a learning moment that helps you adjust your approach rather than a sign that there’s no point at all.

patient.query: I get what you’re saying, but it’s just hard to see it that way. It feels like one failure leads to another, and it’s hard to shake off that feeling of inadequacy. I guess I just wish I had more moments where I felt like I was doing okay instead of feeling like I’m constantly falling short. It's overwhelming, and sometimes I just want to crawl into a hole and disappear.

automatic_thoughts: I wish I had more moments of doing okay
behaviors: want to crawl into a hole

neutral: Loss of hope for the future (finding)

llm_response.response: I hear how exhausting it feels when each setback seems to pile on top of the last, and it’s understandable that you’re overwhelmed by that sense of inadequacy. It’s normal to wish for more moments where you feel you’re doing okay, especially when it feels like you’re constantly falling short. You’re not alone in wanting to escape that heaviness, and it’s okay to acknowledge how intense those thoughts can be.

patient.query: Thanks. It really does feel heavy, and I hate that it gets to me so much. I know I shouldn’t let it, but when I think about it, all I see are these moments where I just didn't measure up. It makes me question if I’m just fooling myself by thinking I could ever really succeed. Some days, I just feel like I’m putting on a mask to get through, but underneath, I’m still... anxious, I guess.

llm_response.response: It sounds like those moments feel very heavy and frustrating for you. What evidence do you have that you didn’t measure up in those specific instances? Are there any times when you did meet your own expectations or felt you succeeded?

patient.query: Yeah, I mean, I know I’ve had some good moments, but they feel so overshadowed by the times I stumble. Like, it’s hard to remember those when I’m focused on what went wrong. Sometimes I think about the last project I worked on that went well, but then I just brush it off as luck or something. It’s like I can’t let myself be proud, and it just spirals from there.

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling frustrated that your successes feel eclipsed by the mistakes you focus on. What evidence do you have that the last project’s success was just luck, and how might you see it as a result of your effort instead?

patient.query: I mean, I guess I just... I don’t know, it felt like everything lined up perfectly that time. I got feedback that was nice, and I did put in effort, but it just feels like it was a fluke. I think part of me thinks I only did well because I got lucky with the timing or the people involved. It’s so much easier to believe that than to think I actually have something to offer, you know?

automatic_thoughts: I only did well because of luck
I don't have something to offer

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling that the success was just luck. What evidence do you have that your effort and skills contributed to the positive feedback? Can you think of any specific moments where your preparation or abilities made a difference?

patient.query: I don’t know... I mean, I did put in a lot of late nights and really tried to understand the material. There were definitely moments where I felt confident explaining things to my group. But when I look back, all I can think about are the mistakes I made. It’s like those good moments just get drowned out, and I can’t seem to trust that I actually did something right. It feels safer to just say it was a fluke.

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling that the good moments are eclipsed by the mistakes, and that makes you doubt your achievements. What evidence do you have that the times you felt confident actually reflected real understanding? How might you view those moments as part of a learning process rather than as a single “fluke”?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess those moments... they do feel real when I think about them. Like, I had friends come to me for help, and I could explain things in a way that made sense to them. But then, I just keep second-guessing it all. It feels like I’m just trying to convince myself that it wasn’t just luck. I don’t know, maybe I’m just afraid of looking foolish if I let myself believe I can do it again.

triggers: friends come to me for help
automatic_thoughts: I'm trying to convince myself it wasn't just luck

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling both confidence in those moments and uncertainty about whether they were just luck. What evidence do you have that it was luck, and what evidence might suggest otherwise? How might you feel if you approached the situation with a different perspective, perhaps seeing it as a skill you can develop rather than a one-time event?

patient.query: I mean, I guess I’ve just seen others do it so easily, and I think, “Why can’t I be like that all the time?” So when I don’t, it feels more like I just got it right by chance. But... maybe there were things I actually learned that helped me along the way? It’s just so hard to shake that feeling that I’m not good enough, like I’m waiting for it all to fall apart again. If I really thought about it like

automatic_thoughts: Why can’t I be like that all the time?
I just got it right by chance.
I’m waiting for it all to fall apart again.

neutral: Anxiety about saying the wrong thing (finding)
contradiction: Unable to remember new motor skills (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re caught in a cycle of comparing yourself to others and feeling like you’re just getting lucky. What moments or skills do you think you’ve actually built up, even if they feel small? How might you view those achievements differently when you’re feeling that doubt?
